pattern: The two allowed moves are exactly the transitions used to generate the reflected binary Gray code from its predecessor, so the number of moves needed to reach zero is the Gray-code rank of n, recoverable in a single downward pass by repeatedly XOR-ing in each shifted-down copy of the remaining bits.
hints: Try tracing the sequence of numbers reachable from 0 using only these two moves in order -- it lists every integer exactly once, each differing from the previous by exactly one bit, which is the defining property of a reflected binary Gray code.
If n is the k-th number to appear in that Gray-code ordering (starting the count at 0 for the value 0 itself), then k is exactly the minimum number of moves to turn n back into 0.

Converting a Gray code back to its ordinary binary rank is a fixed procedure: repeatedly XOR the running result with the current value of n, then shift n one bit to the right, until n becomes 0; the accumulated result is the answer.

edge_inputs.description: smallest positive value, a single set bit at position zero
an isolated single bit well above position zero, easy to under-count if you only handle bit zero specially
a power of two, tempting to treat as trivially costing one move
alternating bit pattern that forces many chained transitions
largest value exercised in this pack's twelve-bit ground-truth range, every bit set
one less than that maximum, adjacent to the all-ones boundary

stress.description: uniformly random n across the full twelve-bit ground-truth range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    hi = max(0, min(size, 4095))
    n = rng.randint(0, hi)
    return [n]

stress.description: random n restricted to small values, where chained low-bit transitions are common
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    hi = max(0, min(size, 63))
    n = rng.randint(0, hi)
    return [n]
